title: Ephemera pockets for journals, making and attaching paper envelopes
subtitle: How to fold, cut, and attach storage pockets for loose inserts
excerpt: Paper pockets tipped into a journal provide a way to store loose ephemera, tickets, pressed flowers, folded notes, stamps, without gluing them down. This tutorial covers two pocket constructions: a simple fold-and-paste pocket and a sewn pocket with a folded closure.

glossaryTerms.definition: Printed or paper items not originally intended for permanent keeping, tickets, receipts, postcards, labels, stamps, and similar flat material. In journalling, ephemera are used as decorative inserts or personal records.
A single-leaf or folded insert attached to a journal page by a thin strip of adhesive along one edge only. A tip-in lies flat in the book but can be lifted and read on both sides.

content.text: Pockets are among the most practical journal additions. A pocket can be a

sheet with three edges glued down and one left open, or a folded card attached to the page. The materials needed are modest: a sheet of card or heavyweight paper, adhesive, and a clean cutting surface.
that goes loose in the pocket stays unfixed and can be swapped out as the collection grows.

items.name: Heavyweight paper or card, 200-300 gsm
items.notes: Thicker stock holds shape better as a pocket. A contrasting colour or pattern makes the pocket a feature of the page rather than a structural afterthought.

items.name: Bone folder
items.notes: For clean, sharp folds on the pocket edges.

items.name: Craft knife and cutting mat
items.notes: For trimming the pocket to size.

items.name: Metal ruler, 30 cm
items.notes: For guiding both fold lines and cuts.

items.name: PVA, acid-free
items.notes: Applied thinly to the three edges being glued down. Avoid getting adhesive inside the pocket opening.

items.name: Paste brush, bookbinding
items.notes: For applying the PVA evenly to the pocket edges.

content.text: Cut a rectangle of card to roughly the width of the journal page and twice the intended pocket depth. Score a fold line across the centre using the bone folder against a metal ruler. Fold the lower half up onto the upper half. Apply a thin line of PVA to both side edges of the folded strip, then press the pocket onto the journal page. Weight it flat for 20 minutes. The upper portion of the rectangle forms the attachment flap; the lower portion forms the pocket itself. Leave the top edge open.

Cut a rectangle of card with the intended pocket height plus an additional 25 mm at the top for the tuck. Score and fold the tuck inward along the 25 mm line. The tuck folds down inside the pocket when loaded. Apply adhesive to the side and bottom edges only and attach to the page. The tuck holds items loosely without gluing them and prevents the pocket contents from slipping out when the journal is held upright.

Pockets work on any page but are most practical near the back of a journal section, where the extra thickness from stored items does not prevent earlier pages from lying flat. A pocket tipped in at the very end of a section adds minimal bulk. Mark the position lightly in pencil before gluing, then erase any visible pencil marks after the adhesive has dried.
